subject: Cannot get my wheelchair from the Metra station to St. Charles Rd
body_clean:
Dear Mayor Okonkwo,

My name is Marcus Webber and I have lived on the 300 block of Bohland Ave for eleven years. I use a power wheelchair full time after a spinal injury, and I commute on the Metra UP-W line from the Bellwood station three or four days a week. I am writing because the sidewalks around the station, particularly along 25th Ave between the platform and St. Charles Rd, are effectively unusable for me, and I have started to feel unsafe.

There are three specific problems. First, at the southwest corner of 25th Ave and St. Charles Rd there is no curb cut at all, just a six-inch vertical lip. To cross I have to roll a block out of my way to Bohland and back. Second, the curb ramp that does exist at the northeast corner has no detectable warning surface (the truncated domes), it is cracked through, and the running slope is so steep my chair's anti-tip wheels catch. Third, the sidewalk panels in front of the old bank building have heaved more than two inches at the joints, which is well above the half-inch ADA threshold I keep reading about.

Last Tuesday around 5:40 PM I was forced into the travel lane on 25th Ave to get around a heaved panel, in the dark, during the evening rush. A driver had to brake hard. I do not think it should take someone getting hit for this to get fixed. I would like to know what the Village intends to do, and whether there is any program to pay for it. I am happy to meet anyone on site to show exactly where the problems are.

Thank you for your time.

subject: Your sidewalk and curb cut concerns near the Metra station
body_clean:
Mr. Webber,

Thank you for writing, and I am sorry it has come to the point where you were forced into traffic on 25th Ave. That is not acceptable and I want it addressed properly, not just patched.

I am copying Tom Reyes, our Village Engineer, and Rosa Marchetti, our Building Official, directly on this message. Tom, I would like a field survey of the sidewalk and the four curb ramps at 25th Ave and St. Charles Rd, plus the run from the Metra platform south to St. Charles, including slope, cross-slope, detectable warning surfaces, and the panel heaves Mr. Webber describes near the old bank building. I want to know what is out of compliance with current PROWAG and ADA standards and what it would cost to bring the whole corridor up to standard, not just spot repairs.

Mr. Webber, I would also like to take you up on your offer to walk the site. Tom will reach out to schedule a time that works for you so the survey reflects exactly where you are running into trouble. The station area is a priority for me because it is a transit access point and the problems you describe affect every resident with a mobility device, a stroller, or a walker, not only you.

I will be candid that a full reconstruction of this kind is usually beyond what we can fund out of the annual street budget, so I am also asking staff to look at outside funding. But first I want the engineering facts in hand. You will hear from Tom this week.

Thank you again for raising this the right way.

subject: Field survey results - 25th Ave / St. Charles Rd ADA deficiencies
body_clean:
Mayor, Rosa, and Mr. Webber (cc),

I walked the corridor with Mr. Webber on November 6 and completed measurements on November 7-8. The findings confirm and extend his report. Summary of the survey:

Curb ramps: Of the four corners at 25th Ave and St. Charles Rd, only one (NE) has a ramp, and it fails on three counts. Running slope measured 11.4 percent against the 8.33 percent maximum; cross-slope at the gutter was 4.1 percent against the 2 percent maximum; and there are no truncated-dome detectable warnings. The other three corners have no ramp at all. PROWAG and the 2010 ADA Standards both require compliant ramps at all four legs.

Sidewalk: From the Metra platform south to St. Charles Rd on the west side of 25th Ave, I logged eleven panel heaves over the 0.5-inch trip-hazard threshold, four of them over 2 inches, and three locations where the clear width drops below 36 inches due to a leaning utility pole and encroaching landscaping. Cross-slope on the walk in front of the former Bellwood Bank building runs 3.5 to 5 percent for roughly 120 feet.

Scope to fix it right: full reconstruction of approximately 1,150 linear feet of 5-foot sidewalk, four new ADA curb ramps with domes, two driveway apron corrections, drainage structure adjustment at the SW corner (the missing ramp also ponds water), and pole relocation coordination with ComEd. My planning-level estimate is $240,000, give or take 15 percent at this stage. This exceeds what the sidewalk program can absorb. I recommend we pursue Cook County CDBG funds; the location qualifies as transit access in what HUD maps show as a low-to-moderate-income block group. I can have a concept layout and cost breakdown ready for a grant application.

subject: ADA standards and interim safety measures for 25th Ave corridor
body_clean:
Mayor, Tom,

I concur with Tom's findings and want to add the regulatory framing so we are on solid ground if this ever gets challenged. The corridor is a pedestrian circulation path in the public right-of-way, so it falls under Title II of the ADA. Under 28 CFR 35.150 and 35.151, when we do an alteration such as the eventual reconstruction, we are required to bring the curb ramps and walk into compliance to the maximum extent feasible. Equally important, the Village is required to have an ADA transition plan that identifies barriers and a schedule to remove them; our current plan dates to 2016 and does not specifically list this corridor. I recommend we add it, because a documented transition plan is exactly what both our liability counsel and a CDBG reviewer will want to see.

On the truncated domes and the 11.4 percent ramp slope at the NE corner, those are not judgment calls; they are measured failures against the 2010 Standards, so I would not want us doing a cheap overlay that leaves them in place. That can actually increase exposure because it counts as an alteration that failed to correct a known barrier.

In the meantime, before any capital project, I would ask Public Works to do three interim things: grind or ramp the worst panel heaves over 2 inches so no one is forced into the lane this winter, trim the landscaping that drops the clear width below 36 inches, and put temporary detectable-warning mats at the NE ramp. These are low cost and they show good faith. They do not replace the full fix, but they protect Mr. Webber and others until the grant comes through.

subject: Moving forward - interim fixes now, CDBG application for the full project
body_clean:
Tom, Rosa, David (cc),

This is exactly the kind of work-up I wanted. Decision on direction:

One, interim safety. Rosa's three measures are approved. I will ask Frank DiMeo to schedule the panel grinding, the landscaping trim, and the temporary warning mats at the NE ramp before the first hard freeze. Please log them in the file so we have a record that the Village acted promptly once it was on notice.

Two, the transition plan. Rosa, please add the 25th Ave / St. Charles corridor to the ADA transition plan as an identified barrier with a target removal year of 2025, contingent on funding. I want that on paper before we apply.

Three, the grant. Tom, proceed with the concept layout and detailed cost breakdown for the full $240,000 reconstruction. We are going to pursue Cook County CDBG. David, I am bringing you in as Finance Director because CDBG will almost certainly require a local match and I need to know what we can commit without blowing the 2025 capital budget. My instinct is to position the local match from the motor fuel tax / Rebuild Illinois sidewalk line so we are not raiding the general fund.

Mr. Webber, please know this is moving. The full reconstruction depends on a grant cycle, so I will not promise you a date for the concrete yet, but the temporary fixes are happening within weeks and the corridor is now formally in our plan. Tom will keep you copied as the application develops.

Let us get the application in for the next county round.

subject: Local match analysis for CDBG ADA sidewalk project
body_clean:
Mayor, Tom,

I have run the numbers on the local match. Cook County's CDBG program for suburban municipalities typically funds public infrastructure with no fixed match requirement on paper, but in practice competitive applications carry 15 to 25 percent local participation, and the county scores leverage. For a $240,000 project I would budget a local share of $48,000 to $60,000 to be competitive, and I would represent it as 20 percent, or $48,000.

Where it comes from: I do not recommend the general fund. We have $63,500 unspent in the FY2025 motor fuel tax line earmarked for sidewalk and curb work, and ADA curb ramps and walks are an eligible MFT use under IDOT's rules. Committing $48,000 of that as the local match leaves a small cushion and keeps the general fund whole. I have confirmed with our auditor that pledging MFT against a CDBG project does not create a supplanting problem because CDBG is the federal source and MFT is state-restricted; they are layered, not duplicated.

Two cautions for the application. First, CDBG is a reimbursement program, so we front the cash and draw down against the county after work is in place and inspected. We need the full $240,000 available in cash flow even though net cost to the Village is the $48,000 match; I can manage that within the capital fund balance. Second, CDBG triggers federal Davis-Bacon prevailing wage on construction, which Tom should build into the engineer's estimate because it raises bid prices roughly 10 to 15 percent over a standard local job. If $240,000 was a non-Davis-Bacon number, we may need to revise upward before we submit.

subject: Revised estimate with Davis-Bacon and CDBG application package ready
body_clean:
Mayor, David, Rosa (cc),

Good catch from David on prevailing wage. My original $240,000 was built on local non-prevailing-wage unit prices. Applying Davis-Bacon rates for the Cook County region and adding the federal contingency, the revised engineer's estimate is $271,500. Breakdown at the concept level: sidewalk removal and replacement, 1,150 LF at $58/LF = $66,700; four ADA curb ramps with truncated domes at $4,800 each = $19,200; drainage structure adjustment and new inlet at the SW corner = $14,500; two driveway apron corrections = $8,800; ComEd pole relocation coordination and allowance = $22,000; pavement and parkway restoration = $19,300; traffic control and Davis-Bacon labor premium = $41,000; mobilization, engineering, and 12 percent contingency = $80,000.

For the application I propose we request $223,500 in CDBG funds and pledge a $48,000 local match from MFT as David outlined, which lands at a 17.7 percent local share. That is within the competitive band and keeps our ask under the county's informal $225,000 cap for a single municipal project in one cycle.

The package is assembled: concept layout sheet, itemized cost estimate, the corridor's inclusion in the updated ADA transition plan (thank you Rosa), the HUD low-to-moderate-income block group documentation showing the area qualifies on both the LMI and the transit-access bases, photographs of the eleven panel heaves and the failed NE ramp, and Mr. Webber's original complaint as documentation of need. The county's spring CDBG application window closes March 14. With your sign-off I will submit by March 7 to leave margin.

subject: Please fund the 25th Ave sidewalk project - I support it too
body_clean:
Dear Mayor Okonkwo,

I heard from my neighbor Marcus Webber that the Village is applying for a grant to rebuild the sidewalks by the Metra station, and I wanted to write so you know it is not just one person who needs this. I am 74 and I use a rollator since my hip replacement two years ago. I take the UP-W into the city to see my grandchildren, and I have stopped using the 25th Ave side entirely because of the broken slabs in front of the old bank. I go the long way around on Bohland, which adds two blocks each way and is hard on me in the cold.

I have watched young mothers bump strollers down into the street at that corner with no curb cut because there is no other way across. It is honestly a little frightening with the rush-hour traffic coming off St. Charles. So this is not only a wheelchair issue, it is everyone who is not steady on their feet or pushing something with wheels.

I do not know much about how grants work, but if it helps for the county to hear from residents, I am glad to sign a letter or come to a meeting. Please do whatever you can to get this funded. A lot of us who depend on that train station would be grateful. Thank you for listening to us.

subject: Application submitted - your letter helps the case
body_clean:
Ms. Brzezinski,

Thank you for writing, and I am glad Marcus told you. Your message could not have been timed better. Our Village Engineer, Tom Reyes, submitted our Cook County CDBG application for this exact project on March 7, ahead of the March 14 deadline. We are requesting $223,500 from the county and committing $48,000 of local funds to rebuild roughly 1,150 feet of sidewalk on 25th Ave from the Metra platform to St. Charles Rd, install four new ADA curb ramps with detectable warnings, fix the drainage at the southwest corner, and correct the heaved slabs in front of the former bank that you described.

You asked whether resident voices help. They do, very much. CDBG scoring rewards demonstrated community need, and a short letter from you describing how the missing curb cuts and broken walk affect you as a rollator user, and what you have seen with strollers and other neighbors, is genuinely useful. I am going to ask Tom to add it to our project file and forward it to the county as supplemental documentation of need. If you are willing, a few specific sentences in your own words carry more weight than anything we could write for you.

I cannot promise the award; it is competitive and the county announces in early summer. But we have put forward a strong, well-documented application, the corridor is now in our ADA transition plan, and the interim safety fixes are already in. You and Marcus pushing on this is the reason it moved. I will make sure you both hear the outcome.

With appreciation for your patience and your willingness to help.

subject: Notice of CDBG Award - Bellwood 25th Ave ADA Sidewalk Improvements
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo and Mr. Reyes,

On behalf of the Cook County Bureau of Economic Development, Community Development Division, I am pleased to provide formal notice that the Village of Bellwood has been selected for a Community Development Block Grant award under our PY2025 suburban allocation for the project titled "25th Avenue ADA Sidewalk and Curb Ramp Improvements."

Award terms: the County has approved $215,000 in CDBG funds against your requested $223,500. The modest reduction reflects our allocation ceiling this cycle and our standard removal of the pole-relocation allowance, which we ask you to coordinate directly with ComEd as a utility cost rather than a grant-eligible line. Your committed local match of $48,000 from motor fuel tax remains as represented and is a factor in the favorable score the project received; the documented community need, including the resident statements you submitted, scored in the top quartile of this round.

Next steps and conditions. This award is contingent on execution of a subrecipient agreement, which my office will send within three weeks. Please note the federal requirements that attach: Davis-Bacon prevailing wage with weekly certified payrolls, Section 3 economic opportunity reporting, an environmental review under 24 CFR Part 58 that must clear before you advertise for bid, and procurement under 2 CFR Part 200. CDBG is reimbursement-based; the Village fronts costs and draws down against documented, inspected work. The performance period runs through December 31, 2026. Please designate a project contact for the environmental review and procurement file. Congratulations to Bellwood; this is a strong project that will make real difference at the station.

subject: We got it - the 25th Ave sidewalk project is funded
body_clean:
Marcus and Ms. Brzezinski,

I wanted you to hear this directly from me before it shows up in the village newsletter. Cook County has awarded Bellwood a Community Development Block Grant for the 25th Avenue ADA sidewalk project. The county approved $215,000, and with our $48,000 local match from the motor fuel tax fund we have the roughly $263,000 we need to do this right. We will rebuild about 1,150 feet of sidewalk from the Metra platform to St. Charles Rd, install four compliant curb ramps with detectable warning domes, fix the drainage at the southwest corner, and remove the heaved slabs in front of the old bank.

The county told us plainly that the resident statements were part of why the application scored in the top quartile. In other words, this happened because Marcus refused to let a near-miss in the travel lane go unreported, and because Ms. Brzezinski took the time to describe what she sees at that corner. That is exactly how this is supposed to work.

I will be honest about timing. There are federal steps before a shovel moves: an environmental review that must clear, prevailing-wage bidding, and the county's procurement rules. Realistically construction lands in the 2026 season, and the grant performance period runs through December 2026. The temporary fixes stay in place until then. Tom Reyes will keep you both posted at each milestone, and I would like to invite you to the ribbon cutting when the new ramps go in.

Thank you for your patience and for pushing. Bellwood is better for it.
